questions.question: A recipe requires 3 cups of flour for every 2 cups of sugar. If a baker uses 15 cups of flour, how many cups of sugar should be used to maintain the same ratio?
questions.explanation: Set up a proportion: $\frac{3 \text{ flour}}{2 \text{ sugar}} = \frac{15 \text{ flour}}{x \text{ sugar}}$. Cross-multiplying gives $3x = 30$, so $x = 10$.

questions.question: An athlete runs at a constant rate of 8 miles per hour. At this rate, how many minutes will it take the athlete to run 2 miles?
questions.explanation: First, find the time in hours: $\text{Time} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Rate}} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25$ hours. Convert hours to minutes: $0.25 \times 60 = 15$ minutes.

questions.question: A floor plan uses a scale where 0.5 inches represents 4 feet. If a room is represented by a length of 3 inches on the plan, what is the actual length of the room in feet?
questions.explanation: Set up a proportion: $\frac{0.5 \text{ in}}{4 \text{ ft}} = \frac{3 \text{ in}}{x \text{ ft}}$. Cross-multiplying: $0.5x = 12$. Dividing by 0.5 (or multiplying by 2) gives $x = 24$.

questions.question: A liquid is flowing through a pipe at a rate of 12 liters per minute. Which of the following is closest to the flow rate in milliliters per second? (1 liter = 1,000 milliliters)
questions.explanation: Convert liters to milliliters: $12 \text{ L/min} \times 1{,}000 = 12{,}000 \text{ mL/min}$. Convert minutes to seconds: $\frac{12{,}000 \text{ mL}}{60 \text{ sec}} = 200 \text{ mL/sec}$.

questions.question: In a certain colony, the ratio of worker ants to soldier ants is 15:2. If there are 450 worker ants, how many total ants (workers and soldiers) are in the colony?
questions.explanation: Find the number of soldiers: $\frac{15}{2} = \frac{450}{x} \Rightarrow 15x = 900 \Rightarrow x = 60$ soldier ants. The total population is $450 \text{ (workers)} + 60 \text{ (soldiers)} = 510$.

questions.question: A printer can produce 45 pages every 3 minutes. At this constant rate, how many pages can the printer produce in 1 hour?
questions.explanation: First, find the rate per minute: $45 \text{ pages} / 3 \text{ minutes} = 15 \text{ pages per minute}$. Since there are 60 minutes in an hour, multiply the rate by 60: $15 \times 60 = 900$ pages.

questions.question: A map has a scale of 1:50,000. If two cities are 12 centimeters apart on the map, what is the actual distance between the cities in kilometers?
questions.explanation: Multiply the map distance by the scale factor: $12 \text{ cm} \times 50{,}000 = 600{,}000 \text{ cm}$. Convert centimeters to meters: $600{,}000 / 100 = 6{,}000 \text{ meters}$. Convert meters to kilometers: $6{,}000 / 1{,}000 = 6 \text{ kilometers}$.

questions.question: In a certain metal alloy, the ratio of copper to zinc is 5 to 3 by weight. If a sample of the alloy contains 12 pounds of zinc, what is the total weight, in pounds, of the sample?
questions.explanation: Set up a proportion for copper ($c$): $\frac{5}{3} = \frac{c}{12}$. Cross-multiplying gives $3c = 60$, so $c = 20$ pounds of copper. The total weight is the weight of copper plus zinc: $20 + 12 = 32$ pounds.

questions.question: An object travels at a speed of 15 meters per second. What is this speed in kilometers per hour?
questions.explanation: Convert meters to kilometers: $15 \text{ m/s} = 0.015 \text{ km/s}$. Convert seconds to hours: $0.015 \text{ km/sec} \times 60 \text{ sec/min} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} = 54 \text{ km/hr}$.

questions.question: A gardener mixes fertilizer with water in a ratio of 2 ounces of fertilizer for every 5 gallons of water. If the gardener uses 12 gallons of water, how many ounces of fertilizer are required?
questions.explanation: Set up the proportion: $\frac{2 \text{ oz}}{5 \text{ gal}} = \frac{x \text{ oz}}{12 \text{ gal}}$. Cross-multiply: $5x = 24$. Divide by 5: $x = 4.8$ ounces.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can confidently set up proportions, convert units across systems, and solve real-world rate problems with precision.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on carefully tracking units during conversions and double-checking that your proportion is set up with matching quantities on top and bottom.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: when setting up a proportion, keep the same units in the same position (e.g., distance/distance = time/time), and always convert units before calculating.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Start by identifying the two quantities being compared, write the ratio clearly, and use dimensional analysis (canceling units) to guide your conversions.
